chapters.title: Chapter 3
chapters.content:
The fire in Elizabeth Corney’s beer shop had burned down to a deep amber glow, throwing long shadows across the low beamed ceiling. The usual crowd had thinned to a handful. James Booker, the village blacksmith, nursing the last of his ale in the corner, two men Edmund half recognised as folk from Angmering, and Elizabeth Corney herself, moving quietly between the tables with a cloth, wiping down surfaces that didn’t particularly need wiping.
	Edmund sat with his back to the wall, a cup of locally brewed ale between both palms. William Jr sat opposite him, his long legs stretched out underneath the table. 
	They hadn’t spoken much since they sat down. This wasn’t unusual after a long day. But tonight, the silence between them had a different texture to it, heavier somehow. 
	Edmund took a slow slip and let his eyes drift around the room. Nothing was wrong, exactly. The fire crackled. Booker coughed into his sleeve. One of the Angmering men murmured something low to the other and they both shifted in their seats. Ordinary enough. And yet, something sat at the back of Edmund’s neck that he couldn’t quite name, a faint prickling.
	Elizabeth set a fresh candle onto the table nearest the door. She caught Edmund’s eye briefly as she passed and gave him a tight, distracted smile of a woman with too much on her mind. He watched her retreat behind the bar and found himself wondering, not for the first time, how much she heard in this room on any given evening. 
	William shifted in his seat and drained the last of his cup.
	“Another?” Edmund asked.
	William shook his head. He turned the empty cup slowly in his hands, round and round, without seeming to notice that he was doing it.
	Outside, the wind had picked up. Edmund could hear it moving through the eaves, a low intermittent moan that rose and fell like breathing. One of the Angmering men stood abruptly, said something brief to his companion, pulled his coat on and left without finishing his drink. The door swung shut behind him and the candle nearest it guttered.
	Edmund watched the remaining man stare into his cup for a long moment before also rising, tossing a coin onto the table and slipping out after his associate.
	The fire settled with a soft collapse of ash. Booker was asleep in the corner now, his chin had dropped to his chest.
	William finally set down his cup and pushed back his chair.
	“I’ll head off,” he said quietly. 
	Edmund nodded. “I won’t be far behind you.”
	William put on his coat and hat, gave Edmund a brief nod, and ducked out through the low doorway. The door closed, sending a short gust of cold wind his way.
	He finished his ale, and took both his and William’s cups to the bar and placed them in front of Elizabeth, so she wouldn’t have to clean up after them.
	“Thank you, Edmund,” said Elizabeth. “You be careful out there.”
	Edmund gave her a quick smile and nodded. He went back over to the table where he and his brother had been sitting and reached for his coat.
	Whatever had been sitting at the back of his neck all evening, had followed him out into the cold.
***
The night had settled hard and cold over the village. The last of the light had since gone from the sky, leaving only a thin crescent moon that offered little more than the suggestion of visibility. He could just make out a series of small plumes of smoke dancing out of chimneys. The hedgerows on either side of the lane were black and shapeless, the fields beyond them had dissolved entirely into darkness.
	His boots crunched steadily on the loose gravel as he turned south toward the Homestead. The village was still. The cottages he passed had shutters drawn, thin threads of firelight bled through the cracks. The wind moved through the bare branches overhead with a sound like whispering.
	He was perhaps about a hundred yards past Corney’s beer shop when he spotted a faint orange light, low and unsteady, flicking between the treeline on the eastern edge of the road, into Homestead land. Not a lantern hung from a post. Something held. Moving slightly, as if passed between hands. He heard voices that were low and distinct, carried unevenly by the wind. He couldn't make out any words, only the rhythm of them. Several men, speaking carefully, the way men do when they do not wish to be heard.
Edmund's first instinct was to walk on. Whatever was happening between those trees was no business of his. He remembered back to his youth when his mother would warn him that curiosity in times like these could cost a man dearly. He took one step forward.
Then the wind shifted, and he caught a single phrase, sharp and clear above the rest.
"The Olliver clan".
He stopped again.
His heart quickened. He stood motionless on the road for a long moment. Then, moving with great care, he stepped slowly toward the low, flint stone wall, crouching down beside it. He moved along the wall until he was close enough that the voices resolved themselves into something approaching coherence.
There were four of them, perhaps five. It was impossible to be certain in the darkness. They were gathered in a loose circle, just inside the treeline, their faces obscured beneath deep hoods and pushed up collars. One of them held the lantern low, close to the ground, so that the light only caught their boots and the lower halves of their coats. Edmund continued to crouch behind the wall, and held his breath to prevent being discovered by the hooded figures.
"The thresher first," one of them said. The voice was low and deliberate, he seemed to speak with authority among the gang. "The machine goes, and the message is sent."
"And if he replaces it?" said another voice, younger and less steady.
"He won't," said the first man. "Not after what follows."
A silence. The lantern swayed slightly.
"There are those among us that I trust with my life. And there are those I trust only as far as their own interests take them. The trick is knowing which is which before it's too late."
Something in that voice nagged Edmund. A familiarity he couldn't place, muffled as it was by the distance. He strained forward slightly, his palms flat against the cold stone wall.
"And the Olliver house itself?" said a third voice, rougher than the others.
A pause that stretched just long enough to be comfortable.
"When the time comes," the first man said, quietly, "the whole clan will know we were here."
The wind dropped for a moment and the voices became clearer, but the men had shifted, turning away from Edmund, and the words continued to be a murmur.
The one holding the lantern turned slightly, and for just a moment, the light caught the lower half of his face beneath the hood. A square jaw, and a particular set to the mouth, thin lipped and down turned. Edmund had seen that mouth before. He was almost certain of it. But the lantern swung back and the darkness swallowed the detail before he could be sure.
One of the figures said something brief and final that Edmund couldn't catch. The group began to disperse, moving slightly in separate directions. Edmund was anxious that he'd be discovered. He pressed up even closer against the wall and held his breath again. He had to sit and hope that they wouldn't see him as they walked off into the distance. Within moments, the light was gone and the treeline was dark and still again, as though nothing had been there at all.
Edmund stayed crouched behind the wall for a long moment after they had gone, his pulse loud in his ears. The wind resumed to its low movement through the branches above him.
He straightened slowly and stood alone on the open road.
He turned the words of the anonymous man's voice over and over in his head.
The thresher first.
This phrase taunted him as he walked on, faster now, his eyes moving instinctively to the shadows on either side of the lane.
The village was as still and silent as it was when he'd left Corney's. The cottages crouched dark and shuttered and the fields beyond stretched away into nothing. Whatever had just passed in those trees, the village knew nothing of it yet. Edmund wasn't sure how much longer that would be true.

chapters.title: Chapter 4
chapters.content:
The pale grey light of a Sunday morning crept through the gap in Edmund's curtains long before he was ready to meet it. He lay still for a moment, staring at the low ceiling, listening to the familiar sounds of the house below. The clatter of a pot. The scrape of a chair. The low rumble of his landlord, James Burcher's, voice, and beneath it, the softer cadence of Mary's reply.
He dressed slowly, his body still carrying the ache of the week's labour, and made his way downstairs.
The kitchen was warm and bright against the grey outside. A good fire burned in the range, and the smell of bacon fat and fresh bread filled the room. Edmund rubbed his hands together at the comfort of a Sunday morning well underway. Mary stood at the stove with her back to him, humming something tuneless and content under her breath. James sat at the head of the table with his broad hands wrapped around a cup of tea, his collar open, with a hearty smile on his face.
"There he is," James said, with a broad grin as Edmund appeared in the doorway. "We thought you might sleep until Monday."
"Leave him alone," said Mary, without turning from the stove. "He works harder than any man in this parish, he deserves all the rest he can get." She turned then, and seeing Edmund's face, immediately reached for a plate. "Sit down love. You look like you need feeding."
Edmund smiled. Mary's soft voice always warmed him. There was something about her that made a man feel looked after, she had a deep attentiveness to the people around her. She had known Edmund since he was a boy, and had known his mother longer still, and yet she treated him as her own. He pulled out a chair adjacent to James' and sat down, whilst James pushed the teapot across the table toward him. "How's your father keeping?" he asked, caringly but with no intention of approaching the subject from aside.
"Holding on," Edmund said, pouring his tea. "He's not himself, but he's okay."
James gave a single nod. A brief moment of silence crept upon the two of them.
"Good man, your father. Always has been," he said giving a smile into space.
Mary set a plate down in front of Edmund containing a heel of bread, a couple of rashers. She rested her soft, gentle hand of his shoulder for a quick moment, before returning to the stove.
Edmund ate in the comfortable quietness, but not awkward.
It was James who eventually broke it. "I saw your William this morning," he said, remembering something which he should've said earlier. "Early. Before light had properly come up."
Edmund looked up from his plate.
"He was moving fast," James continued, "up Sea Road, toward your father's house. Didn't stop to talk."
Edmund set down his fork.
"I didn't want to alarm you," James added, "but he had a look about him. Something not right."
Mary had turned from the stove again, toward the two men. She was watching Edmund with her quiet attentiveness, just as she always did.
"You should go up, love," she said gently. "Finish your breakfast first, mind. But then you should go."
Edmund looked down at his plate, and then back at Mary, then across to James, who gave him a small firm nod of a man who had already made up his mind on the matter, but was waiting for the rest of the room to catch up.
"A'right. I'll head up now and take the rest with me," he said, picking the remaining bit of bread still left on his plate.
He pulled on his coat and boots at the door. Mary handed him a small cloth wrapped parcel of fresh bread as he left.
"For your mother," she said simply.
Edmund smiled and gave her a single nod. "Thank you."
The village was quiet in the way that Sundays always were. The gravel road was empty, the fields on either side still and frost silvered beneath a low grey sky. The church bell had not yet rung for the morning service, and the cottages were still shuttered and unhurried, thin columns of smoke rose straight up in the still air. Edmund walked quickly, he breath clouded ahead of him, the parcel was warm against his palm.
When he got to his father's house he pushed through the gate and front door without knocking.
The front room was small and low ceilinged, crowded with the accumulation of objects of a family that never had quite enough space. A pair of his father's boots sat by the door, worn almost past use, alongside William's who's had been equally as loved. The younger children, Eliza and Maria, sat together on the floor near the fire, their knees drawn up, unusually quiet. They knew something was up as they didn't usually see William Jr and Edmund before church on a Sunday, they looked weary and watched from afar.
Edmund's mother stood at the far end of the room with her arms folded tightly across her chest, her back half turned. She looked around when she heard him come in and he immediately saw that she had been crying, not recently, but not long ago either. He could tell by the redness around her eyes that she had tried to hide, but hadn't managed.
"What's happened?" he said.
Before she could answer, William Jr appeared at the top of the narrow staircase. He came down quickly, ducking his head beneath the low beam that stretched across. His countenance was difficult to read.
"He's had a bad night," William said. "Mother found him on the floor this morning. He'd tried to get up and his legs gave way."
Edmund looked at his mother. She gave a small nod, pressing her lips together.
"He's back in bed now," William continued. "He won't admit how bad it is. You know what he's like."
"Has anyone sent for the doctor?" Edmund asked.
"With what?" his mother said quietly.
Edmund looked over at the two children by the fire. Eliza was staring back at the flames, and the younger one, Maria, was looking back at him.
Edmund crossed the room and placed the parcel of bread on the table. "Mary sent this."
His mother looked at it for a moment, before picking it up. "God bless her," she said softly.
William had moved to the window, with his back to the room, looking out at the frost covered yard.
"I'll go up and see him," Edmund said.
"He'll tell you he's fine," William said, without turning around.
"I know."
He climbed the stairs, only standing on every other step, to get up quicker, with one hand on the wall to balance on the narrow, but steep, steps. The smell of damp wood grew stronger as he reached the top. He pushed open the bedroom door.
His father was propped against a pillow, a blanket drawn up to his chest, despite the warmth of the fire that had been built up in the small grate. He looked smaller than Edmund remembered, diminished somehow against the bedding, though only a handful of days had past since he had last visited his father's bedside. His colour was poor, and his breathing was shallow, but his eyes were open and sharp, and when he saw Edmund in the doorway, he straightened himself slightly, as though determined not to be caught lying down.
"Don't look at me like that," his father said.
"Like what?" Edmund said, pulling a small stool closer to the bed, sitting on it.
"Like I'm already in the ground."
"I wasn't."
His father looked at him for a steady moment, then let out a long, slow breath and settled back against the pillow. "I'll be up in a day or two," he said. "I just need some rest."
"Take all the time you need," replied Edmund.
His father's eyes drifted toward the window. A long silence settled between them, not uncomfortable, the silence of two people who have known each other long enough to not feel the need to fill every moment with words.
"Olliver's been good to us," Edmund said eventually.
His father gave a small nod. "He has."
"The rations will see you, Ma and the kids through while you're not working. And when you're back on your feet there'll be work waiting."
His father said nothing for a moment. Then, without looking at Edmund, he said, "Your brother doesn't see it that way."
Edmund held his gaze at the window. "I know."
"He's angry," his father said. "He's always been angry. Even as a boy." A pause.
Edmund nodded slowly. He stayed there a while longer, until his father's eyes grew heavy and breathing deepened into something closer to sleep. Then, he rose quietly and lifted the stool back into the corner of the room, and went back downstairs.
William was sat by the window, looking out. He heard Edmund walking down the stairs and spoke to him without turning to look.
"And for how long are we going to keep relying on Olliver's charity?"
"It isn't charity," Edmund said. "He's being fair."
William scoffed. "Fair. A fair man pays a fair wage. A fair man doesn't hold a machine over your head like a threat."
"He hasn't threatened anyone."
William grumbled and mumbled something that Edmund couldn't quite make out. Edmund pulled his coat straight and looked at his brother for a long moment. William held his gaze outside, at this point a number of families could be seen walking past the house, most likely on the way to the Sunday morning church service.
"Just leave it alone for now, William. For Pa."
William said nothing.
Edmund let himself out into the brisk morning and stood for a moment on the path, the church bell had begun its slow toll across the rooftops of the village. He stood and listened to it, and thought about his deteriorating father, alongside William's attitude towards Olliver's generosity. He turned his collar up and begun his walk to the church.
